bids.reasoning: As this is the opening offer at the very beginning of the negotiation, I will start by proposing my most preferred values for all issues. This serves to maximize my potential utility and sets a strong initial anchor from which I can steadily make considered concessions in subsequent rounds.

The opponent started with the lowest utility offer possible (all valueA) and attempted to artificially manufacture urgency via their message, despite the time fraction being extremely early (t=0.02). I will ignore their fake urgency and cheap talk. To keep the process moving, I will make a genuine but small concession on my lowest-priority issue (issueA) while holding completely firm on my highest-priority issues (issueC and issueD).

The current time fraction is very low (t=0.04), yet the opponent is employing a fake urgency tactic claiming time is running out. They have twice offered their maximum utility bid, which yields minimal utility to me. Since it is still early in the negotiation, I will ignore their pressure tactics and make another small concession on my lowest-priority issue (issueA) to demonstrate a willingness to move, while keeping my most important issues (issueD, issueC, and issueB) at their maximum value.

The opponent's message attempts to create fake urgency despite it being very early in the negotiation (t=0.06). While they made a small concession on `issueD` to `valueB`, `issueD` is my most important issue, so I will continue to hold firm on it at `valueF`. To show willingness to negotiate without significantly damaging my overall utility, I will make a minor concession on `issueB` (my second lowest weighted issue) while keeping `issueC` and `issueD` at maximum value.

The current time fraction is very early in the negotiation (t=0.08) despite the opponent's repeated messages creating false urgency and threatening deadlock. I will ignore the psychological pressure and focus on the numbers. The opponent's slight concession on my highest-weight issue (issueD) suggests they may have flexibility there. My strategy is to hold firm on my most important issues (issueC and issueD) while making a small concession on my lowest-weight issue (issueA) to keep the negotiation moving and signal flexibility.

The opponent is displaying fake urgency tactics, as the time fraction is only 0.1, so we have plenty of time. I am targeting an overall utility of approximately 0.89 to gradually approach a compromise while maintaining my top priorities. I am holding firm on issueC and issueD, which are the most heavily weighted for me, while making another concession on issueB to encourage reciprocity.

The current time fraction is very early (t = 0.12), so the opponent's messages claiming that the window is rapidly closing are fake urgency tactics. I will ignore them and concede slowly on my least important issues (issueA and issueB), while remaining firm on my highest-priority issues (issueC and issueD, which together make up 70% of my total utility). My strategy is to gradually find common ground where it costs me the least.

The current time is very early (t=0.14) despite the opponent's aggressive messages claiming fake urgency, which I will ignore. The opponent has started making minor concessions on issueD and issueC, which are my highest-weighted issues, while remaining completely fixed on valueA for issueA and issueB. I will capitalize on this apparent difference in preferences by fully conceding to their top value on my lowest-weighted issue (issueA) while holding firm on the issues that matter most to me (issueC and issueD).

The opponent is consistently using false urgency tactics ("window is closing fast", "deadlock") even though we are very early in the negotiation at t=0.16. I am safely ignoring these messages. Since my highest weights are on issueD (0.4) and issueC (0.3), I will hold firm on their top values (valueF) for now. I will make a slight concession on issueB (from valueD to valueC) while keeping my smallest issue (issueA) at the opponent's preferred valueA, aiming for an overall utility of 0.824.

The opponent is aggressively using false urgency tactics ("this window is closing faster than you think," "we're roughly approaching a deadlock") despite the actual time fraction being very early at t=0.18. I am completely ignoring these pressure statements and will instead pace my concessions reasonably. Given the opponent previously offered `valueB` for `issueB`, I will concede to their proposal on this lower-priority issue while holding firm on `issueC` and `issueD` to preserve my highest-utility values.

We are still very early in the negotiation at t=0.20, despite the opponent's repeated fake urgency tactics in their messages. Looking at their offer history, they are slowly rotating minor concessions on single issues while anchored heavily to valueA across the board. I will hold firm on my highest-priority issue (issueD) while slightly conceding on issueC down to valueE to see if introducing flexibility there will prompt a more meaningful concession, targeting a utility around 0.77 for myself.

The current time fraction is only 0.22; therefore, I can completely disregard the opponent's aggressive, repetitive "fake urgency" tactics attempting to force an early deal. Reviewing the opponent's bidding history, they've shown a willingness to concede on issueA and issueB (having bid "valueB" on these in the past), which aligns perfectly with my strategy since these are my lowest-weight issues. I will offer "valueB" on issues A and B while holding firm on my most highly-weighted issues, C and D, at "valueF", targeting a high utility of roughly 0.80 for myself.

The opponent is aggressively employing a fake urgency tactic, repeatedly claiming that the time window is closing despite the fact that we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.24). I will disregard this cheap talk. Instead, I see they consistently demand `valueA` across the board, occasionally conceding on my most valuable issues, `issueC` and `issueD`. To push towards an agreement while maintaining high utility, I will fully concede my two least important issues (`issueA`, `issueB`) to their preferred `valueA`, but strictly hold firm on obtaining maximum utility on my most important issues (`issueC`, `issueD`).

I am maintaining a high utility (around 0.76) because the time fraction is only 0.26, so there is no real urgency despite the opponent's repeated claims of impending deadlock. Since my weights for issueC and issueD are highest (0.3 and 0.4), I will hold firm on high values for those issues (valueE and valueF). However, they have shown movement by offering `valueB` on `issueB` and making concessions on `issueC`. I will incorporate their latest concession on issueD (valueE) but demand maximum utility on issueC in return, while keeping issueA at valueA and issueB at valueB to respect their apparent priorities.

I will match their recent concessions on issueA and issueB, while optimizing for my own utility by targeting valueF on issueC and valueE on issueD, yielding a robust utility of 0.741 while exploring a new combination.

Seeing that the opponent is willing to concede on issueA (up to valueC) and issueC (up to valueE), I am making a matching concession on issueA to valueC while holding firm on issueD and issueC to ensure my utility stays around 0.77.

However, their bidding behaviour shows genuine concession on my most valuable issue (issueD), as they recently offered valueE. I will incorporate valueE for issueD and valueA for issueA to align with their strong preferences, while pulling issueC back to my highest utility value to maintain a solid overall payout margin for this stage of the negotiation.

In exchange, I will demand high values for my most critical issues, issueC and issueD, aiming for a utility of ~0.73.

The opponent is consistently using fake urgency tactics ("Time is running out", "deadlock") to pressure me, but since we are only at t=0.5, I will ignore these false constraints. Examining the offer history, I see the opponent is willing to concede on issueD (offering valueE at t=0.46) and issueC (offering valueE at t=0.34, 0.42, 0.50) but remains rigid on issueA and issueB.
